# **Ontological and Mereological Taxonomy of Generative Architecture**

To effectively decompile the algorithmic architecture of world-class, long-form generative art—specifically the brutalist algorithmic structures present in Ben Kovach’s *Edifice* collection 1—it is a strict mathematical and philosophical necessity to first establish the mereological framework governing the digital canvas. Traditional computational geometry relies on a top-down, reductionist set theory: the canvas is the absolute "whole," and the coordinates are its subordinate "elements." The Eros engine categorically rejects this naive hierarchy. Elite generative architectural forms do not emerge from subdividing a master canvas; they emerge from the absolute ontological autonomy of their constituent parts.2

This analytical framework is grounded in "Mereological Tectonics" and Object-Oriented Ontology (OOO).2 Drawing upon the architectural theory of Daniel Koehler and his analysis in *The Mereological City* 5, a generative structure must be defined strictly by its part-to-part relationships rather than part-to-whole subordination.2 In a computational space, an algorithm must not impose a predefined "whole" (e.g., a static global composition). Instead, the whole is entirely absent, existing only as the terminal accumulation of microscopic, local coordinate interactions.2 Following Tristan Garcia’s ontology, an object within the canvas—a single grid cell, a solitary plotted vector, or a bounding box—is defined as "a thing limited by other things and conditioned by one or several things".2

In visualizing this philosophical shift from part-to-whole to part-to-part mereology, one must conceptualize a network diagram mapping these relationships, where autonomous generative entities (lines, cells, forces) interact exclusively through extrinsic local relations without a defining outer border, representing the total absence of the 'whole' canvas. In the context of Kovach's *Edifice*, the coordinate space is discretized into millions of these "Ultimate Parts".2 A singular crosshatched line is not merely a stroke rendering; it is an autonomous entity possessing its own localized physical properties, including momentum, base phase, and trajectory.2 When this line encounters the boundary of its parent grid cell—the "Enclosure"—it does not reference the master canvas for rendering instructions. It interacts directly, part-to-part, with the boundary matrix. As articulated by Manuel DeLanda’s Assemblage Theory, these are strictly "extrinsic relations," where parts connect, reflect, and react without losing their individual mathematical identities or being subsumed by a dictatorial global composition.2

This mereological approach mathematically eliminates the concept of "empty space." Space is not a void waiting to be filled; it is an active, participating computational entity—a bounding geometry that asserts physical forces (attraction, repulsion, friction) onto the ink vectors traversing it.8 Every ![img][image1] (N x N) monolithic block generated by the stochastic packing algorithm 9 operates as an independent sovereign state. When Kovach applies global displacement matrices (such as the affine "Sharp" or "Squish" transformations) 8, he is not merely warping a rasterized image; he is manipulating the underlying spatial logic of the universe the entities inhabit. The parts warp congruently because their existence is inextricably bound to the topology of the space they occupy.

The aesthetic of "eroding architecture" 11 is thus not simulated via post-processing image filters; it is mechanically derived from the friction between autonomous objects (integration lines) colliding with strict mathematical borders (enclosures) under the influence of global spatial shearing. The tension is literal, mathematically calculated at every fractional integration step, forging a brutalist visual output from the pure ontic collisions of its ultimate parts.

## **The Unknown Unknowns of Computational Geometry**

To achieve the elite tier of generative architectural synthesis, one must progress beyond the established canon of continuous noise functions (Perlin, Simplex) and rudimentary collision algorithms.12 The modern generative landscape is heavily bounded by "known knowns"—established geometric limits that algorithms are explicitly trained or programmed to handle. However, the pursuit of true brutalist emergence requires the deliberate invocation and mathematical management of "unknown unknowns".13

In generative architecture, an unknown unknown manifests when an autonomous system scales beyond its programmed geometric parameters, creating topological densities or structural phase transitions that the artist could not mathematically foresee.15 When deploying N-Descending random walks combined with non-linear repulsor fields 9, the exact path of the integrated vector becomes highly sensitive to initial conditions. Small perturbations in the PRNG sequence cascade through the numerical integration steps 18, generating localized physical attractors that trap ink lines in infinitely compounding feedback loops. Without intervention, this results in unpredictable topological collapse—dense "black holes" of computational ink that destroy the mereological autonomy of the grid.

To mitigate and harness these unknown unknowns, advanced computational geometry employs principles derived from Topological Data Analysis (TDA).19 TDA relies on algebraic topology, specifically persistent homology and simplicial complexes, to map the fundamental "shape" of the data rather than its exact spatial coordinates.19 In the context of the Eros engine, the generative canvas can be analyzed topologically in real-time. Instead of executing naive, computationally expensive ![][image2] distance checks between millions of strokes (as seen in basic continuous flow-field collision routines) 21, the engine calculates the Betti numbers (![][image3]) of the localized grid clusters.19 ![][image4] counts the number of connected components, while ![][image5] identifies 1-dimensional topological holes or cyclic feedback loops.

By measuring the persistent homology of the stroke vectors as they accumulate, the algorithm gains "objective awareness" of the topological density.19 If a localized area approaches infinite density (a generative unknown unknown resulting from compounding inverse-square repulsor forces), the system detects the topological collapse before rendering and adjusts the localized integration limits dynamically. This methodology allows the generative architecture to safely deploy highly volatile, unstable physics equations—such as ![][image6]\-body gravitational matrices or hyper-dimensional flow fields—knowing the geometric constraints will dynamically restrict the stroke rendering based on pure topological stability rather than rigid, arbitrary line-count limits. This fusion of autonomous mereological part generation with real-time topological analysis forms the absolute frontier of long-form computational abstraction.

## **Comparative Algorithmic Paradigms: Hobbs, Naon, and Kovach**

To fully decompile Kovach's *Edifice*, its algorithmic structure must be rigorously mapped against its contemporaries in the elite long-form generative space, specifically Tyler Hobbs (*Fidenza*) and Manolo Gamboa Naon (*PPRLL*, *PPRDD*).22 Each artist represents a distinct mereological and mathematical paradigm.

Tyler Hobbs' *Fidenza* fundamentally rejects the discrete spatial partitioning utilized by Kovach. Hobbs relies on a continuous, unconstrained flow field driven largely by Perlin noise gradients.11 The algorithm's core constraint mechanism is not an absolute geometric boundary, but rather rigorous spatial hashing and radial collision detection. The algorithm checks distances between vectors (dist(a,b,e,h) \<= c+i) to ensure neighboring curves never overlap.21 This produces an organic, fluid mereology where the "parts" (lines) negotiate their existence based on proximity rather than enclosure. Hobbs’ approach is intrinsically biological and continuous, prioritizing non-destructive flow.11

Conversely, Manolo Gamboa Naon (*Manoloide*) operates through intense computational plasticity and maximalist density.22 His work explores the potential of programming as a purely expressive language, drawing heavily on 20th-century aesthetic blends (Kandinsky, Delaunay).26 Naon's algorithms utilize extreme recursive layering, cellular noise, and color synthesis, pushing the canvas to the limits of visual saturation.23 His mereological state is one of over-partition and over-overlap 2, where geometric boundaries exist but are intentionally obfuscated by millions of additive micro-strokes.

Kovach’s *Edifice* stands apart through its strict adherence to brutalist physics and discrete mereological taxonomy.8 It rejects the continuous proximity checks of Hobbs and the organic noise of Naon, relying instead on hard mathematical matrices, fractional integration, and absolute topological clamping.

| Paradigm Attribute             | Tyler Hobbs (Fidenza)                       | Manolo Gamboa Naon                       | Ben Kovach (Edifice)                           |
| :----------------------------- | :------------------------------------------ | :--------------------------------------- | :--------------------------------------------- |
| **Mereological State**   | Continuous / Proximity-based 25             | Over-lapped / Plastic 2                  | Discrete / Part-to-Part Autonomous 2           |
| **Spatial Partitioning** | None (Global continuous field) 11           | Recursive Subdivision / Cellular 23      | N-Descending Stochastic Matrix Grid 8          |
| **Primary Vector Force** | Perlin / Simplex Noise Gradients 25         | Chaotic Attractors / Complex Rulesets 22 | Affine Matrices & Inverse-Square Torsion 29    |
| **Constraint Logic**     | Radial Collision Detection (![][image2]) 21 | Additive Density Saturation 27           | Absolute Topological Clamping (Math.min/max) 8 |
| **Render Emulation**     | Painterly / Analog Brushwork 30             | Digital Plastic / High-Chroma Vector 22  | Eroded Blueprint / Mechanical Plotter 1        |

By isolating these variables, the cold, hard logic required to replicate the brutalist tension of *Edifice* within the Eros Custom JavaScript Engine becomes clear: continuous noise must be abandoned, spatial arrays must dictate bounds, and rigid mathematical physics must rule the canvas.

### ---

**1\. Aesthetic Diagnostics**

Ben Kovach’s Edifice is a brutalist collision of rigid algorithmic architecture and relentless entropic decay, evoking the tactile density of distressed blueprints eroding under violent physical forces.1 The artistic tension relies on the extreme dichotomy between a mathematically rigid macro-composition—built via stochastic grid packing—and a micro-texture forged from millions of overlapping, non-noise-driven vector crosshatches that simulate the physical bleed and imperfection of an over-driven mechanical plotter.8

### **2\. Algorithmic Decompilation**

* **Layout/Pack Strategy (The Pack):** N-Descending Random Walk Grid Packing. Space is discretized into a high-resolution integer matrix ![][image7] where contiguous rectangles of decreasing scale (from massive ![][image1] monolithic structures down to ![][image8] micro-shrapnel) are aggressively packed.9 The algorithm steps recursively from adjacent filled coordinates, evaluating a validation function against the existing matrix data to ensure zero dimensional overlap. A pseudo-random threshold (![][image9]) is injected into the validation loop, strictly eliminating dead space while entirely rejecting naive, uniform mathematical modularity.31 This intentional injection of failure forces asymmetrical architectural variance, juxtaposing vast sovereign enclosures directly against fractured computational shrapnel.8
* **Generative Forces (The Net):** Global Matrix Displacement via Non-Noise Vector Mechanics. Post-packing, the coordinate plane is subjected to custom mathematical distortion matrices. Affine transforms—like parity-based row skewing (Kovach's "Sharp" logic, derived as ![][image10] based on ![][image11]) and trapezoidal mappings (Kovach's "Squish" logic)—violently shear the underlying geometry.8

![][image12]

Superimposed upon these affine shears are localized inverse-square repulsor fields (![][image13]) that calculate radial torsion based on exact spatial positions relative to distributed point masses.17 The system strictly rejects generic, fluid Perlin noise, ensuring all spatial warping is the direct result of calculated mechanical fatigue and mathematically derived spatial torsion.12

* **Constraint Logic (The Enclosure):** Pre-Displacement Absolute Topological Clamping. Generative crosshatching lines are strictly clamped to their parent bounding boxes (the arrays defined in The Pack) using continuous Math.max() and Math.min() intersection checks prior to the global warp. This implements Kovach's "Modern" boundary style, creating sticky, inelastic collisions where vector velocity reaches absolute zero upon edge contact.8 As the overarching displacement function distorts the global canvas space, the internal coordinate physics warp congruently. Because the clamping occurs computationally prior to the affine displacement matrices, the system ensures the geometry shatters and skews without bleeding ink across unassigned structural fault lines, maintaining the absolute mereological independence of every enclosure.2
* **Render Technique (The Hatch):** Fractional Kernel-Smoothed Crosshatching via Fourth-Order Runge-Kutta. True volumetric depth is built entirely in 2D by rasterizing over 20 million ultra-fine, low-opacity strokes utilizing composite blending (ctx.globalCompositeOperation \= 'multiply'). A localized discrete flow field drives the stroke paths.8 Crucially, the mathematical spatial derivatives must be calculated using Fourth-Order Runge-Kutta (RK4) integration rather than naive Euler integration. Euler steps accumulate catastrophic discretization error across tight inverse-square torsion curves, resulting in numerical instability.18 RK4 samples the derivative four times per fractional step, guaranteeing that the thousands of sub-pixel overlapping segments trace their geometric trajectories flawlessly. This achieves extreme tactile density and physical plotter emulation without relying on 3D illumination models or shader post-processing.18

![][image14]

### **3\. Implementation Strategy**

JavaScript

// EROS ENGINE \- MEREOLOGICAL TECTONICS AND RK4 HATCHING KERNEL
// STRICT DEPENDENCY: PRNG Seed Matrix, Float64Array for Sub-pixel Precision

/\*\*
 \* 1\. THE PACK: N-Descending Topological Space Partitioning
 \* Rejecting naive array subdivision in favor of unrolled Int8 matrices
 \* to guarantee raw cache speed and absolute spatial sovereignty.
 \*/
function allocateTopologicalGrid(cols, rows, maxScale) {
    const grid\= new Int8Array(cols \* rows);
    const enclosures\=;

    // Descending iteration forces the juxtaposition of monolithic spaces against microscopic shrapnel
    for (let N\= maxScale; N \>= 1; N--) {
    for (let i\= 0; i \< cols \* rows; i++) {
    if (grid\[i\]\!== 0) continue;

    const x\= i % cols;
    const y\= Math.floor(i / cols);

    // O(n^2) spatial collision check within the immediate discrete matrix
    if (x\+ N \<= cols && y \+ N \<= rows) {
    let valid\= true;
    for (let dy\= 0; dy \< N; dy++) {
    for (let dx\= 0; dx \< N; dx++) {
    if (grid\[(y \+ dy) \* cols \+ (x \+ dx)\]\!== 0) valid \= false;
    }
    }

    // Generative PRNG threshold (0.42) aborts perfect packing, forcing structural decay
    if (valid && (prng.next()\> 0.42 |

| N \=== 1)) {
    for (let dy\= 0; dy \< N; dy++) {
    for (let dx\= 0; dx \< N; dx++) {
    grid\[(y \+ dy) \* cols \+ (x \+ dx)\] \= 1;
    }
    }
    // Generate the autonomous mereological object with localized mathematical identities
    enclosures.push({
    x, y, w: N, h: N,
    phase: prng.next()\* Math.PI \* 2,
    friction: prng.next()\* 0.5 \+ 0.5 // Coefficient for 'Explosive' bounding states
    });
    }
    }
    }
    }
    return enclosures;
}

/\*\*
 \* 2\. THE NET: Global Affine Warping & Local Repulsor Physics
 \* Combining the 'Squish' matrix displacement with Inverse-Square Torsion.
 \* This function bypasses Perlin noise entirely.
 \*/
function applyMereologicalDisplacement(px, py, cx, cy, repulsors) {
    let dx\= px \- cx;
    let dy\= py \- cy;

    // Torsion logic: Evaluating local repulsor nodes using inverse-square metrics (F\= m / (e \+ r^2))
    let torsionX\= 0;
    let torsionY\= 0;
    for (let i\= 0; i \< repulsors.length; i++) {
    let rx\= px \- repulsors\[i\].x;
    let ry\= py \- repulsors\[i\].y;
    let distSq\= rx \* rx \+ ry \* ry;

    let force\= repulsors\[i\].mass / (15.0 \+ distSq);
    torsionX\+= rx \* force;
    torsionY\+= ry \* force;
    }

    // Affine 'Squish' Logic: Parity-based horizontal scaling creates trapezoidal structural shears
    const rowParity\= Math.floor(py \* 0.04) % 2;
    const squishScale\= (rowParity \=== 0)? 1.4 : 0.6;

    // Affine 'Sharp' Logic (Optional augmentation): px\+= (rowParity \=== 0)? 2.5 : \-2.5

    return {
    x: cx\+ (dx \* squishScale) \+ torsionX,
    y: cy\+ dy \+ torsionY
    };
}

/\*\*
 \* 3\. THE HATCH: Fourth-Order Runge-Kutta (RK4) Vector Integration
 \* Strict mathematical demand: Euler integration is rejected due to rapid
 \* discretization error accumulation in dense generative curves.
 \*/
function rk4Step(x, y, phase, stepSize) {
    // Underlying localized discrete flow derivative function (Euler equations)
    const derivative\= (lx, ly) \=\> {
    return {
    vx: Math.cos(phase)\+ Math.sin(ly \* 0.02),
    vy: Math.sin(phase)\+ Math.cos(lx \* 0.02)
    };
    };

    // Calculate the four fractional slopes
    const k1\= derivative(x, y);
    const k2\= derivative(x \+ 0.5 \* stepSize \* k1.vx, y \+ 0.5 \* stepSize \* k1.vy);
    const k3\= derivative(x \+ 0.5 \* stepSize \* k2.vx, y \+ 0.5 \* stepSize \* k2.vy);
    const k4\= derivative(x \+ stepSize \* k3.vx, y \+ stepSize \* k3.vy);

    // Compute the weighted average for the final spatial coordinate
    return {
    nx: x\+ (stepSize / 6) \* (k1.vx \+ 2 \* k2.vx \+ 2 \* k3.vx \+ k4.vx),
    ny: y\+ (stepSize / 6) \* (k1.vy \+ 2 \* k2.vy \+ 2 \* k3.vy \+ k4.vy)
    };
}

/\*\*
 \* 4\. THE ENCLOSURE: Rendering the Emergent Tectonics
 \* Executing topological clamping and multiplicative plotter physics.
 \*/
function renderBrutalistTectonics(ctx, enclosures, globalRepulsors, W, H) {
    // Emulate architectural blueprint plotter density via composite multiplication
    ctx.globalCompositeOperation\= 'multiply';
    ctx.lineWidth\= 0.45;

    for (let enc of enclosures) {
    // Map discrete grid array back to float-space canvas coordinates
    const rx\= enc.x \* (W / 64);
    const ry\= enc.y \* (H / 64);
    const rw\= enc.w \* (W / 64);
    const rh\= enc.h \* (H / 64);

    // Mass-dependent scale: Smaller spaces suffer exponentially higher generative densities
    const mass\= enc.w \* enc.h;
    const lineCount\= Math.floor((15000 / Math.sqrt(mass)) \* prng.next());

    // Low-opacity analog pigment generation
    ctx.strokeStyle\= \`hsla(215, 30%, 25%, 0.04)\`;

    ctx.beginPath();
    for (let i\= 0; i \< lineCount; i++) {
    let lx\= rx \+ prng.next() \* rw;
    let ly\= ry \+ prng.next() \* rh;

    let warpedStart\= applyMereologicalDisplacement(lx, ly, W/2, H/2, globalRepulsors);
    ctx.moveTo(warpedStart.x, warpedStart.y);

    for (let step\= 0; step \< 40; step++) {
    // Execute precise RK4 Integration for the structural crosshatch
    let nextPos\= rk4Step(lx, ly, enc.phase, 2.0);
    lx\= nextPos.nx;
    ly\= nextPos.ny;

    // THE ENCLOSURE: Strict mathematical clamp prior to global displacement
    // Sticky 'Modern' topological bounds prevent fault-line ink bleeding
    if (lx\< rx |

| lx \> rx \+ rw |
| ly \< ry |
| ly \> ry \+ rh) break;

    // Route the valid, constrained vector through the Affine global physics matrix
    let warpedPoint\= applyMereologicalDisplacement(lx, ly, W/2, H/2, globalRepulsors);
    ctx.lineTo(warpedPoint.x, warpedPoint.y);
    }
    }
    ctx.stroke(); // Accumulate physical pigment density
    }
}

#### **Works cited**
